songContext.summary: A swaggering, aggressive track mixing boastful masculinity and violent street imagery with flirtatious/dangerous attraction. The narrator and his crew present themselves as armed, untouchable and charismatic — their looks ('knife brows') and actions 'kill' or dominate, while social-media/status references and local cultural touches (churma, reels) punctuate the braggadocio. Recurrent ideas: weapons and threat, deadly charm, loyalty/crew presence, and proud, confrontational self-presentation.
notablePhrases: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
Dil cheer ke gaye hain, teer tikkhe eyebrow
Reel dekhdekh teri main muka di saari GB
Meri groza mein barood, dekh baahar aa reya butt
Raakhan aise hathiyar, gale dodo magazine
Tanne jitni quality ginaayi ae main, chhori (chhori = girl / dear girl)
Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan

lines.original: Romeo
lines.literal: Romeo
lines.natural: Romeo
lines.slangAware: Romeo (the lover/charmer)
lines.chosen: Romeo
lines.translated: Romeo
lines.note: Transliteration: Romeo. Persona/label for a charming/romantic man; literal transfer.
lines.selectorReason: Direct, literal match; preserves intended persona.

lines.original: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan
lines.literal: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan
lines.natural: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.slangAware: Her gaze is loaded — the girl's got death in her eyes.
lines.chosen: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.translated: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.note: Transliteration: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan. 'chhori' = girl. Conservative reading kept ('loaded with the stuff of death').
lines.ambiguity: 'Chaali bhar ke' is idiomatic and exact nuance is unclear.
lines.selectorReason: Most semantically faithful while remaining conservative about the regional idiom.

lines.original: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
lines.literal: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
lines.natural: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.slangAware: He owns hearts, prowling like the Taliban.
lines.chosen: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.translated: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.note: Transliteration: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban. 'dilaan' = hearts. Kept literal metaphor of fear/dominance.
lines.ambiguity: Subject (he/I/we) not explicit in line; 'Taliban' used metaphorically for domination.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative literal rendering that preserves metaphor and meaning.

lines.original: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
lines.literal: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
lines.natural: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.slangAware: They speak low, kill without even firing a shot.
lines.chosen: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.translated: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.note: Transliteration: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan. Could be literal threat or figurative (charm); translated conservatively as killing without shooting.
lines.selectorReason: Accurate to wording and tone; preserves the double-edged threat/charisma reading.

lines.original: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
lines.literal: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
lines.natural: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.slangAware: The boys turned into walking corpses; the city's become a funeral ground.
lines.chosen: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.translated: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.note: Transliteration: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan. 'zinda laash' = living corpse; literal metaphor kept.
lines.selectorReason: Clear, direct translation with strong semantic match.

lines.original: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan
lines.literal: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan
lines.natural: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.slangAware: Her gaze is loaded — the girl's got death in her eyes.
lines.chosen: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.translated: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.note: Transliteration: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan. 'chhori' = girl. Conservative reading kept ('loaded with the stuff of death').
lines.ambiguity: 'Chaali bhar ke' is idiomatic and exact nuance is unclear.
lines.selectorReason: Most semantically faithful while remaining conservative about the regional idiom.

lines.original: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
lines.literal: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
lines.natural: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.slangAware: He owns hearts, prowling like the Taliban.
lines.chosen: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.translated: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.note: Transliteration: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban. 'dilaan' = hearts. Kept literal metaphor of fear/dominance.
lines.ambiguity: Subject (he/I/we) not explicit in line; 'Taliban' used metaphorically for domination.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative literal rendering that preserves metaphor and meaning.

lines.original: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
lines.literal: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
lines.natural: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.slangAware: They speak low, kill without even firing a shot.
lines.chosen: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.translated: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.note: Transliteration: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan. Could be literal threat or figurative (charm); translated conservatively as killing without shooting.
lines.selectorReason: Accurate to wording and tone; preserves the double-edged threat/charisma reading.

lines.original: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
lines.literal: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
lines.natural: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.slangAware: The boys turned into walking corpses; the city's become a funeral ground.
lines.chosen: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.translated: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.note: Transliteration: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan. 'zinda laash' = living corpse; literal metaphor kept.
lines.selectorReason: Clear, direct translation with strong semantic match.

lines.original: Saara jagg liya thug, phaink husan ka jaala
lines.literal: Saara jagg liya thug, phaink husan ka jaala
lines.natural: I took the whole world — I threw a net of beauty.
lines.slangAware: I claimed the whole world, cast a web of beauty.
lines.chosen: I took the whole world — I threw a net of beauty.
lines.translated: I took the whole world — I threw a net of beauty.
lines.transliteration: Tera chehra nasha maaf kare Mexico aala
lines.note: Transliteration: Saara jagg liya thug, phaink husan ka jaala. 'phaink ... jaala' = cast a net; translated conservatively.
lines.ambiguity: 'thug' could be vocative or part of the boast; left as boastful claim.
lines.selectorReason: Preserves bragging sense and metaphor (casting a net of beauty).

lines.original: Tera chehra nasha maaf kare Mexico aala
lines.literal: Tera chehra nasha maaf kare Mexico aala
lines.natural: Your face's intoxication — even Mexico would forgive.
lines.slangAware: Your face is intoxicating — even Mexico would pardon it.
lines.chosen: Your face's intoxication — even Mexico would forgive.
lines.translated: Your face's intoxication — even Mexico would forgive.
lines.transliteration: Dil cheer ke gaye hain, teer tikkhe eyebrow
lines.note: Transliteration: Tera chehra nasha maaf kare Mexico aala. Conservative literal rendering used despite cultural ambiguity.
lines.ambiguity: 'Mexico aala' is idiomatic/ambiguous; exact intended reference unclear.
lines.selectorReason: Chooses the literal candidate but flags low confidence due to unclear idiom.

lines.original: Dil cheer ke gaye hain, teer tikkhe eyebrow
lines.literal: Dil cheer ke gaye hain, teer tikkhe eyebrow
lines.natural: They ripped hearts apart — eyebrows sharp as arrows.
lines.slangAware: They tore out hearts; eyebrows sharp like arrows.
lines.chosen: They ripped hearts apart — eyebrows sharp as arrows.
lines.translated: They ripped hearts apart — eyebrows sharp as arrows.
lines.transliteration: Beauty natural teri, poora face pe glow
lines.note: Transliteration: Dil cheer ke gaye hain, teer tikkhe eyebrow. 'teer tikkhe' = sharp like arrows; kept imagery intact.
lines.selectorReason: Faithful to violent/visual metaphor linking hearts and sharp brows.

lines.original: Beauty natural teri, poora face pe glow
lines.literal: Beauty natural teri, poora face pe glow
lines.natural: Your beauty's natural, your whole face is glowing.
lines.slangAware: Natural beauty — your whole face got that glow.
lines.chosen: Your beauty's natural, your whole face is glowing.
lines.translated: Your beauty's natural, your whole face is glowing.
lines.note: Transliteration: Beauty natural teri, poora face pe glow. Straightforward, literal-modern mixing of English words handled directly.
lines.selectorReason: Direct and accurate; preserves register mixing.

lines.original: Reel dekhdekh teri main muka di saari GB
lines.literal: Reel dekhdekh teri main muka di saari GB
lines.natural: Watching your reels, I burned through all my GB (data).
lines.slangAware: Been staring at your reels — I ate up all my GB.
lines.chosen: Watching your reels, I burned through all my GB.
lines.translated: Watching your reels, I burned through all my GB.
lines.note: Transliteration: Reel dekhdekh teri main muka di saari GB. 'muka di saari GB' interpreted as using up data while watching reels.
lines.ambiguity: 'GB' likely = mobile data (gigabytes); could be other initials but context supports data.
lines.selectorReason: Context (reels) makes data interpretation most accurate and natural.

lines.original: Dil ban ya trophy, re, tu bani RCB
lines.literal: Dil ban ya trophy, re, tu bani RCB
lines.natural: Be it my heart or my trophy — you turned into RCB.
lines.slangAware: Heart or trophy — girl, you turned into RCB.
lines.chosen: Be it my heart or my trophy — you turned into RCB.
lines.translated: Be it my heart or my trophy — you turned into RCB.
lines.note: Transliteration: Dil ban ya trophy, re, tu bani RCB. Kept literal RCB reference rather than inventing metaphor.
lines.ambiguity: RCB is a cultural reference (cricket team); intended connotation unclear so left literal.
lines.selectorReason: Preserves cultural reference; conservative literal choice due to ambiguity.

lines.original: Aankh shiny, step chhori kar rahi ae katt
lines.literal: Aankh shiny, step chhori kar rahi ae katt
lines.natural: Her eyes are shining, the girl’s got a killer step.
lines.slangAware: Eyes shining — the girl's flexin' a killer step ('katt' = sharp/cool).
lines.chosen: Her eyes are shining, the girl’s got a killer step.
lines.translated: Her eyes are shining, the girl’s got a killer step.
lines.note: Transliteration: Aankh shiny, step chhori kar rahi ae katt. 'katt' rendered as 'killer' for lyrical flow and likely meaning.
lines.ambiguity: 'katt' is slangy (sharp/cutting/killer); nuance varies regionally.
lines.selectorReason: Balances literalness and slangy feel while keeping consistent tone.

lines.original: Meri groza mein barood, dekh baahar aa reya butt
lines.literal: Meri groza mein barood, dekh baahar aa reya butt
lines.natural: There's gunpowder in my Groza — look, the butt's sticking out.
lines.slangAware: My Groza's packed with powder — peep the butt/stock sticking out.
lines.chosen: There's gunpowder in my Groza — look, the butt's sticking out.
lines.translated: There's gunpowder in my Groza — look, the butt's sticking out.
lines.note: Transliteration: Meri groza mein barood, dekh baahar aa reya butt. 'Groza' = gun model; rendered accordingly.
lines.ambiguity: Slight uncertainty whether 'butt' refers to weapon stock or a body part; weapon reading fits context.
lines.selectorReason: Weapon-context translation best matches surrounding violent imagery; conservative wording chosen.

lines.original: Note itne ke shehar tere peedan pe ni patte
lines.literal: Note itne ke shehar tere peedan pe ni patte
lines.natural: So many banknotes that your city's trees don't have leaves.
lines.slangAware: Stacks so big, the city's trees got no leaves left — only notes.
lines.chosen: So many banknotes that your city's trees don't have leaves.
lines.translated: So many banknotes that your city's trees don't have leaves.
lines.note: Transliteration: Note itne ke shehar tere peedan pe ni patte. Preserves metaphor of abundance of cash.
lines.ambiguity: Metaphorical image; interpreted as money replacing leaves.
lines.selectorReason: Maintains metaphor and bragging tone; literal-metaphor kept for clarity.

lines.original: Pehrun aaj bhi main yaaran gera maange hoye latte
lines.literal: I still wear today my friends asked-for 'latte'
lines.natural: Even today I still wear the pair my friends begged me for.
lines.slangAware: I still rock the kicks my mates used to beg me for.
lines.chosen: Even today I still wear the pair my friends begged me for.
lines.translated: Even today I still wear the pair my friends begged me for.
lines.note: Transliteration: Pehrun aaj bhi main yaaran gera maange hoye latte. 'latte' rendered conservatively as 'pair' (shoes/kicks).
lines.ambiguity: 'latte' unclear (likely shoes); exact item uncertain.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative choice given uncertain slang; keeps intended meaning (wearing sought-after item).

lines.original: Age athaara te dhare hain sir maamle sangeen
lines.literal: After eighteen there are cases on head, matters serious
lines.natural: Past eighteen, there are cases on heads; the matters are serious.
lines.slangAware: Once you're past eighteen, cases land on your head — things get heavy.
lines.chosen: Past eighteen, there are cases on heads; the matters are serious.
lines.translated: Past eighteen, there are cases on heads; the matters are serious.
lines.note: Transliteration: Age athaara te dhare hain sir maamle sangeen. Refers to age 18 and legal/trouble cases (dhare = charges/records).
lines.selectorReason: Accurate, literal rendering that preserves warning/seriousness of line.

lines.original: Raakhan aise hathiyar, gale dodo magazine
lines.literal: We keep such weapons, around neck two magazines
lines.natural: We carry weapons like that — two magazines hanging round the neck.
lines.slangAware: We rock weapons like that — two mags hanging off the neck.
lines.chosen: We carry weapons like that — two magazines hanging round the neck.
lines.translated: We carry weapons like that — two magazines hanging round the neck.
lines.note: Magazine = ammunition clip; visible extra magazines around the neck.
lines.selectorReason: Accurate literal rendering; preserves image of weapons and two magazines; reads naturally in English.

lines.original: Road baap ka maan ke lyaanva sadkaan pe gaadi
lines.literal: Road father's pride taking (it) street on car
lines.natural: Proud like the boss of the road, we bring our car out on the streets.
lines.slangAware: Like road bosses — we roll our car out on the streets.
lines.chosen: Proud like the boss of the road, we bring our car out on the streets.
lines.translated: Proud like the boss of the road, we bring our car out on the streets.
lines.note: 'baap' used as 'boss/king'; 'lyaanva' rendered conservatively as 'bring/bring out'.
lines.ambiguity: Minor uncertainty about exact tense/form of 'lyaanva' (bring/drive out).
lines.selectorReason: Keeps the boastful 'road boss' meaning and the act of driving/bringing the car onto the street; conservative tense choice.

lines.original: Bahar karaan kaam jinki ni ghar mein ajaadi
lines.literal: Outside we do work whose not in home there's freedom
lines.natural: We do outside work for those whose homes don't have freedom.
lines.slangAware: We handle outside jobs for people who ain't free at home.
lines.chosen: We do outside work for those whose homes aren't free.
lines.translated: We do outside work for those whose homes aren't free.
lines.note: Conservative reading: performing 'outside' work on behalf of people lacking freedom at home.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear who 'jinki' refers to and exact nuance of 'ajaadi' (freedom) — could mean restricted families or those lacking funds/freedom.
lines.selectorReason: Literal/conservative translation chosen because context is ambiguous; avoids inventing specifics about whom they serve.

lines.original: Leke chaale bade daale, gear marji ke ghale
lines.literal: Taking (we) go big stacks, gear as-will in 'ghale'
lines.natural: We move with big stacks, shifting gears however we want.
lines.slangAware: We roll with heavy stacks, switching gears on our terms.
lines.chosen: We move with big stacks, shifting gears however we want.
lines.translated: We move with big stacks, shifting gears however we want.
lines.note: 'bade daale' = large sums/stacks; 'gear' likely literal or metaphor for tempo/control.
lines.ambiguity: Some local word ('ghale') unclear; interpreted as control/pace (gear).
lines.selectorReason: Preserves both wealth ('big stacks') and the mobility/control image ('shifting gears'), matching surrounding lines.

lines.original: Aage bamhana ka chhora, paachhe baithe hain do laale
lines.literal: Up front a bamhana boy, behind sit two laale
lines.natural: There's a 'bamhana' boy up front, two lads sitting behind him.
lines.slangAware: Up front's a 'bamhana' kid, got two boys riding shotgun.
lines.chosen: There's a 'bamhana' boy up front, two lads sitting behind him.
lines.translated: There's a 'bamhana' boy up front, two lads sitting behind him.
lines.note: Kept 'bamhana' as-is because meaning isn't clear from context.
lines.ambiguity: 'bamhana' unclear (nickname/role); left untranslated to avoid guessing.
lines.selectorReason: Maintains original term to avoid misrepresenting a possible proper name or local slang while keeping scene-setting intact.

lines.original: Jutti jhaad ke rakhein hain, bairi saad ke
lines.literal: We have taken off shoes, for the enemy's sake
lines.natural: We've kicked off their shoes, to humiliate the enemy.
lines.slangAware: We strip 'em of their shoes, humiliate the foe.
lines.chosen: We've kicked off their shoes, to humiliate the enemy.
lines.translated: We've kicked off their shoes, to humiliate the enemy.
lines.note: Interprets the action as an insult/humiliation performed on enemies.
lines.ambiguity: Could alternatively mean 'we keep our shoes clean', but surrounding lines suggest humiliation of foes.
lines.selectorReason: Context (threat/taunt verses enemies) supports the humiliating interpretation; selected for semantic fit.

lines.original: Hala dyan jote naadke re beecho beech paad ke
lines.literal: Hala's shoes tied/naadke placed right in the middle
lines.natural: We tie Hala's shoes and put them smack in the middle.
lines.slangAware: We tie up Hala's shoes and drop 'em right in the centre.
lines.chosen: We tie up Hala's shoes and put them right in the middle.
lines.translated: We tie up Hala's shoes and put them right in the middle.
lines.note: High uncertainty; translation kept literal pieces rather than inventing meaning.
lines.ambiguity: 'Hala' may be a name/nickname; 'naadke' likely 'tied/knotted' — overall referent and purpose unclear.
lines.selectorReason: Literal, conservative rendering chosen because the phrase contains unclear/localized terms; flagged low confidence.

lines.original: Raakhe do numberi aa sandh, mora kar dena band
lines.literal: Keeps two 'do-numberi' around, stop making a 'mora'
lines.natural: He keeps two shady ones with him — cut out the show.
lines.slangAware: He's got two fake ones on his side — stop the act.
lines.chosen: He keeps two shady ones with him — stop the show.
lines.translated: He keeps two shady ones with him — stop the show.
lines.note: 'do-numberi' = two-faced/shady people; 'mora' read as 'act/show' to fit tone.
lines.ambiguity: Uncertain meaning of 'mora' — interpreted conservatively as 'show/act'.
lines.selectorReason: Preserves idea of someone surrounding himself with shady people and a command to cut the act; natural English phrasing.

lines.original: Bore batti raakhe khet ke main gaad ke
lines.literal: Bore fuse/light I keep and bury in the field
lines.natural: I plant the fuse/light in the field.
lines.slangAware: I stake the fuse/light in the field, bury it.
lines.chosen: I plant the fuse/light in the field.
lines.translated: I plant the fuse/light in the field.
lines.note: Possible violent implication kept but wording conservative due to ambiguity.
lines.ambiguity: 'bore batti' could be literal light or a fuse/igniter; translated as 'fuse/light' to stay neutral.
lines.selectorReason: Keeps the agricultural/weaponized image without overstating specifics about the object.

lines.original: Khaanvaana churma, re, jama desi baane mein milenge
lines.literal: To eat: churma, hey, we'll meet gathered in desi ways
lines.natural: We'll eat churma — we'll meet up in our desi way.
lines.slangAware: We'll chow down churma — meet up proper desi-style.
lines.chosen: We'll eat churma — we'll meet up in our desi way.
lines.translated: We'll eat churma — we'll meet up in our desi way.
lines.note: Churma = Punjabi sweet; 'jama desi baane' = meeting in a traditional/desi manner.
lines.selectorReason: Clear cultural reference; natural, accurate English that preserves tone.

lines.original: Dand maarde akhaade mein, na to thaane mein milenge
lines.literal: We give beatings in the akhada, otherwise you'll meet us at the police station
lines.natural: We beat folks in the akhada; otherwise you'll run into us at the police station.
lines.slangAware: We square things in the ring — otherwise you'll be seeing us at the cops.
lines.chosen: We beat folks in the akhada; otherwise you'll run into us at the police station.
lines.translated: We beat folks in the akhada; otherwise you'll run into us at the police station.
lines.note: 'akhada' = fighting ring/wrestling arena; also implies physical confrontation leading to police.
lines.ambiguity: Line intentionally contrasts fighting in the akhada and encountering police; reading kept both options.
lines.selectorReason: Conveys both consequences in the original line, matching the boastful threat.

lines.original: Tanne jitni quality ginaayi ae main, chhori
lines.literal: As much quality as you've counted in me, girl
lines.natural: Girl, you've counted how much quality I have.
lines.slangAware: Girl, you've sized up how much quality I got.
lines.chosen: Girl, you've counted how much quality I have.
lines.translated: Girl, you've counted how much quality I have.
lines.note: Chhori = affectionate address to a girl.
lines.selectorReason: Direct, idiomatic English preserving the speaker-address and boast about 'quality'.

lines.original: Tanne aise chhore bas Haryane mein milenge
lines.literal: You'll find such boys only in Haryana
lines.natural: You'll only find boys like this in Haryana.
lines.slangAware: You only get boys like this back in Haryana.
lines.chosen: You'll only find boys like this in Haryana.
lines.translated: You'll only find boys like this in Haryana.
lines.note: Regional identity (Haryana) emphasized as source of the speaker's type.
lines.selectorReason: Straightforward, accurate translation that keeps local pride intact.

lines.original: Maal baba ki daya te, nyu ae chaalenge sapaate
lines.literal: By Maal Baba's mercy, we'll walk like this, flat/straight
lines.natural: By Maal Baba's grace, we'll walk like this.
lines.slangAware: By the boss's blessing, we'll be strutting like this.
lines.chosen: By Maal Baba's grace, we'll walk like this.
lines.translated: By Maal Baba's grace, we'll walk like this.
lines.note: Conservative rendering avoids speculating on the exact identity of 'Maal Baba'.
lines.ambiguity: 'Maal Baba' could be a person, patron, or slang for money/connection; left as-is.
lines.selectorReason: Faithful to the original while preserving possible double-meaning; natural English.

lines.original: Set kare ve trend jyon ni kise pe bhi paate
lines.literal: They set trends like nobody else gets
lines.natural: They set trends like nobody else can.
lines.slangAware: They set trends like no one else can touch.
lines.chosen: They set trends like nobody else can.
lines.translated: They set trends like nobody else can.
lines.note: Clear boast about trendsetting.
lines.selectorReason: Accurate and idiomatic; preserves comparative emphasis.

lines.original: Arey, bas kar tai aale dukhaan ki na puchhe
lines.literal: Hey, stop it — don't ask about those shops over there
lines.natural: Hey, stop it — don't ask about those shops.
lines.slangAware: Yo, chill — don't be asking about those joints over there.
lines.chosen: Hey, stop it — don't ask about those shops.
lines.translated: Hey, stop it — don't ask about those shops.
lines.note: 'dukhaan' = shops/joints; tone is dismissive/censoring curiosity.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative, natural phrasing matching the warning tone.

lines.original: Log phaansi kha jya karein seh reya jitne main ghaate
lines.literal: People get hanged for as many losses I took.
lines.natural: People would be hanged for the losses I suffered.
lines.slangAware: People'd hang for the losses I've taken.
lines.chosen: People would be hanged for the losses I suffered.
lines.translated: People would be hanged for the losses I suffered.
lines.note: Figurative expression implying extreme hardship/penalty tied to speaker's losses.
lines.ambiguity: Unclear particles/conditional structure in original, but figurative meaning preserved.
lines.selectorReason: Keeps strong figurative sense without inventing unstated qualifiers.

lines.original: Tere naam chadhwa dyun, billo, AK santaali
lines.literal: I'll get it raised in your name, billo, AK santaali.
lines.natural: I'll put an AK in your name, babe.
lines.slangAware: I'll get an AK put under your name, billo.
lines.chosen: I'll put an AK in your name, babe.
lines.translated: I'll put an AK in your name, babe.
lines.note: 'billo' = affectionate address to a woman; the line implies gifting/registering a weapon under her name.
lines.ambiguity: 'santaali' unclear (possible model or rhyme) — omitted to avoid guessing.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative, idiomatic English that preserves the provocative gesture while excluding unclear tag.

lines.original: Tanne goliyan ki kholan ki bana ke de doon baali
lines.literal: I'll make a box/case of bullets for you and give it, girl.
lines.natural: I'll make you a box of bullets and give it to you, girl.
lines.slangAware: I'll hook you up with a box of bullets, girl.
lines.chosen: I'll make you a box of bullets and give it to you, girl.
lines.translated: I'll make you a box of bullets and give it to you, girl.
lines.note: Maintains violent/braggadocious offer; phrasing chosen for naturalness.
lines.ambiguity: 'kholan' likely means 'case' or 'box' — selected 'box of bullets' for clarity.
lines.selectorReason: Accurately conveys the idea of arming/gifting ammunition in plain English.

lines.original: Tanne apni bana leyan, tere gel ki ne saali
lines.literal: I'll make you mine; your 'gel' is my saali.
lines.natural: I'll make you mine — your girl will be my 'saali' (sister-in-law).
lines.slangAware: I'll make you mine; your girl'll be my 'saali.'
lines.chosen: I'll make you mine — your girl will be my 'saali'.
lines.translated: I'll make you mine — your girl will be my 'saali'.
lines.note: Kept 'saali' untranslated with brief parenthetical in previous drafts; here preserved in quotes to flag cultural meaning.
lines.ambiguity: 'gel' is ambiguous (likely 'girl'); tone is colloquial/insulting — 'saali' literally sister-in-law used provocatively.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative choice that preserves the original lewd/boastful claim while marking an uncertain term and avoiding over-interpretation.

lines.original: Taur itni bana dyaan, kade jaani ni sambhaali
lines.literal: I'll make such attitude, never been handled before.
lines.natural: I'll put on such an attitude that no one has ever managed it.
lines.slangAware: I'll flex so hard, nobody's ever handled it.
lines.chosen: I'll put on such an attitude that no one has ever managed it.
lines.translated: I'll put on such an attitude that no one has ever managed it.
lines.note: transliteration: Taur itni bana dyaan, kade jaani ni sambhaali. 'taur' = attitude/stance.
lines.ambiguity: The phrase 'kade jaani ni sambhaali' is idiomatic and can be interpreted several ways (never been handled/never been matched); rendered conservatively.
lines.selectorReason: Preserves original boast about an unmatched swagger; chosen for semantic accuracy and conservative idiomatic rendering rather than adding extra slang.

lines.original: Ke ke karoon main bayaan, uthe dil mein toofan
lines.literal: What can I say in words — there's a storm in my heart.
lines.natural: How can I describe it — there's a storm in my heart.
lines.slangAware: What can I even say — my heart's a storm.
lines.chosen: How can I describe it — there's a storm in my heart.
lines.translated: How can I describe it — there's a storm in my heart.
lines.note: transliteration: Ke ke karoon main bayaan, uthe dil mein toofan.
lines.selectorReason: Direct, natural English that keeps the metaphor and intensity of the original without adding or diluting meaning.

lines.original: Reh wah chup bhi ni ho rahi, meri khule na zubaan
lines.literal: I can't even stay quiet, my mouth won't remain closed.
lines.natural: I can't stay quiet — my mouth won't keep shut.
lines.slangAware: I can't be silent — my mouth won't shut.
lines.chosen: I can't stay quiet — my mouth won't keep shut.
lines.translated: I can't stay quiet — my mouth won't keep shut.
lines.note: transliteration: Reh wah chup bhi ni ho rahi, meri khule na zubaan.
lines.selectorReason: Straightforward, idiomatic translation that preserves the original sense (unable to be silent).

lines.original: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan
lines.literal: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan
lines.natural: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.slangAware: Her gaze is loaded — the girl's got death in her eyes.
lines.chosen: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.translated: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.note: Transliteration: Chaali bhar ke naina mein chhori maut ka samaan. 'chhori' = girl. Conservative reading kept ('loaded with the stuff of death').
lines.ambiguity: 'Chaali bhar ke' is idiomatic and exact nuance is unclear.
lines.selectorReason: Most semantically faithful while remaining conservative about the regional idiom.

lines.original: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
lines.literal: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban
lines.natural: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.slangAware: He owns hearts, prowling like the Taliban.
lines.chosen: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.translated: He seizes hearts — roaming around like a Taliban.
lines.note: Transliteration: Kare kabje dilaan pe, bani phire Taliban. 'dilaan' = hearts. Kept literal metaphor of fear/dominance.
lines.ambiguity: Subject (he/I/we) not explicit in line; 'Taliban' used metaphorically for domination.
lines.selectorReason: Conservative literal rendering that preserves metaphor and meaning.

lines.original: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
lines.literal: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan
lines.natural: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.slangAware: They speak low, kill without even firing a shot.
lines.chosen: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.translated: They whisper softly — they take lives without firing a bullet.
lines.note: Transliteration: Bole haulihauli, bina goli maare kheeche jaan. Could be literal threat or figurative (charm); translated conservatively as killing without shooting.
lines.selectorReason: Accurate to wording and tone; preserves the double-edged threat/charisma reading.

lines.original: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
lines.literal: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan
lines.natural: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.slangAware: The boys turned into walking corpses; the city's become a funeral ground.
lines.chosen: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.translated: The guys became living corpses; the city turned into a crematorium.
lines.note: Transliteration: Chhore zinda laash bane, shehar banya shamshaan. 'zinda laash' = living corpse; literal metaphor kept.
lines.selectorReason: Clear, direct translation with strong semantic match.

lines.natural: The girl's eyes are loaded with the stuff of death.
lines.slangAware: Her gaze is loaded — the girl's got death in her eyes.

lines.selectorReason: Most semantically faithful while remaining conservative about the regional idiom.

lines.original: Nyoliwala this side, baby
lines.literal: Nyoliwala this side, baby
lines.natural: Nyoliwala's on this side, baby.
lines.slangAware: Nyoliwala this side, baby — I'm here, watch out.
lines.chosen: Nyoliwala's on this side, baby.
lines.translated: Nyoliwala's on this side, baby.
lines.note: transliteration: Nyoliwala this side, baby. 'this side' = present/claiming presence.
lines.selectorReason: Simple, idiomatic rendering of a bragging presence line; maintains original name and swagger.
